data.title: Eli Lilly, Amgen tout U.S. investments as Trump imposes 100% pharma tariffs
data.description:
Hours after President Donald Trump imposed 100% tariffs on pharmaceuticals with exemptions for companies building manufacturing plants in the U.S., Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) and Amgen (NASDAQ:AMGN) announced their latest investments in their home country.

Lilly announced the opening of its latest Lilly Gateway Labs in San Diego, California, making the site the Indiana-based drugmaker’s fourth shared innovation hub, adding to three other locations in South San Francisco and Boston. 

The 82,514-square-foot facility, part of LLY's global network of LGL sites, is expected to provide lab space for up to 15 life sciences firms, allowing them to share expertise with Lilly (NYSE:LLY).

Meanwhile, Amgen (NASDAQ:AMGN) announced plans to scale up a production site in Puerto Rico as part of a $650M investment to expand its U.S. manufacturing network.

The investment is focused on enhancing technology and boosting the company’s biologics drug production at a facility in Juncos, the California-based drugmaker said, adding that it is expected to generate roughly 750 jobs in manufacturing, construction, and other operations.

Earlier in the day, Swiss drugmakers Novartis (NVS) and Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) made similar claims as EU pharma stocks came under pressure following Trump’s surprise decision, which is set to take effect on Oct. 1, exempting companies building new manufacturing sites in the U.S.

Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) cited an Aug. 25 announcement to indicate that its U.S.-based Genentech unit had broken ground for a new plant in Holly Springs, North Carolina, and the company has already made a $50B pledge to expand its manufacturing and R&D footprint in the U.S.

Novartis (NVS) said that Trump’s latest tariff threat will not impact the company's operations. "We have ongoing construction and expect to announce five new sites to be under construction before end of year," Reuters reported, quoting a company statement.

data.title: Furniture tariffs split industry between heavy importers, domestic brands
data.description:
President Trump’s announcement to hit all imported finished furniture with tariffs is just the latest blow to the industry’s struggles to remain profitable in an environment of high inflation, cautious consumers, and supply chain inefficiencies.

On Thursday, Trump sent reverberations through the sector with the announcement that imports of finished upholstered furniture along with kitchen and bathroom cabinetry and vanities would be subject to a 30% and 50% tariff, respectively.

Losses were more pronounced among sourcing-heavy retailers with RH (NYSE:RH), Wayfair (NYSE:W), and Williams Sonoma (NYSE:WSM) taking the brunt.

But given the industry’s sensitivity to the housing market and trends in discretionary spending weaknesses in either is already impacting the financial health of furniture sales.

Dependence on inexpensive foreign labor makes them particularly vulnerable to U.S. trade shifts. Since the bulk of finished products are sourced from China and Vietnam (with smaller amounts from Malaysia, Indonesia, and Italy), the new 30% import tariff threatens to erode profitability and margins.

Those that are most impacted by the recent development include RH (NYSE:RH) with roughly half of its finished furniture imported, Lovesac (NASDAQ:LOVE) which imports finished products from Vietnam and Malaysia, and to a lessor extend, China, and Natuzzi (NYSE:NTZ) which brings its lower-end finished furniture from China, and higher-end from Italy and Southeast Asia.

On the flip side are those positioned to benefit from the shifting competitive landscape shaped by U.S. trade policy

With nearly all its furniture assembled in the U.S., the announcement of new tariffs drove shares of La-Z-Boy (NYSE:LZB), Bassett Furniture (NASDAQ:BSET), Ethan Allen Interiors (NYSE:ETD), and Hooker Furniture (NASDAQ:HOFT) all higher with Ethan Allen’s (NYSE:ETD) reaction snapping a five-day losing streak.

While the market reaction illustrates the knee-jerk reaction to tariff announcements, furniture retailers have had time to prepare. Trump announced late August that he intended to hit foreign-made furniture with tariffs after a 50-day review.

“I am pleased to announce that we are doing a major tariff investigation on furniture coming into the United States,” Trump wrote on his Truth Social platform last month, adding that, “Within the next 50 days, that investigation will be completed, and furniture coming from other countries into the United States will be tariffed at a rate yet to be determined,” he added.

Some anticipated the risk by shifting manufacturing out of Asia early on and into places like North Carolina, Virgina, and Tennessee, as well as Mexico and Canada which remain protected by the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA), while others like Ethan Allen (ETB) began the shift as far back as 2019, reaching a goal of 70%+ American-made by 2025.

For investors, the policy shift is a reminder that tariff headlines can quickly redraw winners and losers in an industry already battling high costs and weak demand.

data.title: Duke Energy downgraded at Jefferies, saying time to exit with 'victory lap complete'
data.description:
Duke Energy (NYSE:DUK) +0.2% in Friday's trading despite receiving a downgrade to Hold from Buy with a $134 price target at Jefferies, after the utility completed its "balance sheet victory lap with FFO/debt target achieved and regulatory milestones cleared," and saying the successful execution is now fully reflected in the stock price.

Duke Energy's (NYSE:DUK) credit, legislation and transaction successes are real, with equity needs substantially pre-funded through 2027-29, Jefferies' Julien Dumoulin-Smith said, but the analyst does not see near-term catalysts moving 2025-29 earnings higher, as he already forecast a 6.3% 2025-29 EPS compound annual growth rate and a 7.5% rate base compound annual growth rate.

Brookfield's minority investment in Duke Energy Florida (DUK) validates the utility's value while providing capital flexibility for incremental growth, South Carolina proceedings appear "completely copacetic," and constructive North Carolina multi-year rate plan mechanics and tracker mechanisms reduce regulatory lag, Dumoulin-Smith said.

data.title: Hillenbrand gains on report that it's set to collect final bids
data.description:
Hillenbrand (NYSE:HI) rose 6.1% on a report that the plastics maker is expected to collect final bids for its sales process next week.

Apollo Global Management (APO), Lone Star Funds, Stellex Capital Management are among the final suitors for Hillenbrand (NYSE:HI), according to a Dealreporter item on Friday, which cited people familiar with the matter. Financing for a potential deal is in place. 

Hillenbrand and Apollo declined requests for comment from Dealreporter. Lone Star Funds and Stellex did not respond to requests for comment.

The latest update comes after Bloomberg reported last month that Hillenbrand was company is evaluating its strategic options, including a possible sale.

data.title: SoftBank, Ark said to be in talks for Tether's big funding round
data.description:
SoftBank Group (OTCPK:SFTBY) (OTCPK:SFTBF) and Cathie Wood's Ark Investment Management are potential investors in a funding round that seeks to raise $15B-$20B for a 3% stake in stablecoin giant Tether, according to a media report on Friday.

The company behind the world's largest stablecoin Tether (USDT-USD) could be valued at as much as $500B as a result, making it one of the largest private companies globally.

Attracting high-profile investors like SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBY) and Ark would go a long way in bolstering Tether's credibility among mainstream investors, Bloomberg News reported, citing people familiar with the matter. The company has been seeking to attain political legitimacy after it had previously been under scrutiny in the U.S. for Tether's (USDT-USD) alleged role in illicit financing.

data.title: Las Vegas gaming revenue impresses in August after big jumps for baccarat and sportsbooks
data.description:
Gaming win on the Las Vegas Strip increased in August amid concerns about a slowdown in the gambling mecca.

Statewide gaming win was up 5.5% year-over-year in August to $1.23 billion. The Las Vegas Strip saw gaming rise 5.5% to $679.4 million for the typically slow month. Downtown Las Vegas casinos saw gaming win rise 8.4% during the month to $63.3 million. Meanwhile, Reno casinos saw gaming win increase 4.9% during the month to $68.6 million.

Across the state, gaming win was up 2.2% for the slot machine business on a win percentage of 6.3%. Table/counter/card game gaming win shot up 12.6% on a strong win percentage of 15.7%. Baccarat revenue soared 55% during the month to easily offset a decline in roulette revenue. Gaming win at sportsbooks rose 68% year-over-year off a huge jump in early football betting.

Las Vegas tourism trends in general have been soft this year, with visitor volume, hotel occupancy rates, average daily room rates, and airport passenger traffic all down compared to a year ago. High resort fees and rising service prices are considered a major factor, as well as softer international tourism trends than a year ago.

Sector watch: Caesars Entertainment (CZR), MGM Resorts (NYSE:MGM), Boyd Gaming (BYD), Wynn Resorts (WYNN), Full House Resorts (FLL), Bally's Corporation (BALY), Sportradar (SRAD), Genius Sports (GENI), and Red Rock Resorts (RRR).

data.title: Wall Street rises after PCE index data comes in line
data.description:
Wall Street rises on Friday after the Federal Reserve’s preferred measure of inflation gauge showed results in line with expectations, easing investor concerns over price pressures and bolstering market sentiment.

The blue-chip Dow (DJI) has pushed up by 0.6%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) is +0.4%, and the tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) trades higher by 0.1%.

From a sector-by-sector stance, eight of the 11 S&P groups are in positive trading territory, with Energy leading the way. At the other end of the spectrum, Info Tech has suffered the most. 

U.S. Treasury yields remain mostly unchanged. The longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y)  is up 1 basis point to 4.19% and the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) is flat at 3.66%.

On the economic front, the Core Personal Consumption Expenditure Price Index (excludes food and energy) came in at +0.2% vs. +0.2% consensus and +0.2% prior (revised from +0.3%), and +2.9% Y/Y vs. +2.9% consensus and +2.9% in July. 

The PCE Price Index came in at +0.3% vs. +0.3% consensus and +0.2% prior, and +2.7% Y/Y vs. +2.7% consensus and +2.6% prior. 

“Friday's PCE for August was as expected, and although it is still firmly above the Federal Reserve's 2% target, we believe the central bank remains on track to cut interest rates again at its next meeting in October, since it's clear that inflation is stable enough to handle lower interest rates,” Clark Bellin, president and chief investment officer of Bellwether Wealth stated. 

Furthermore, the September U.S. Consumer Sentiment Index also arrived. 

On tariff news, President Donald Trump announced sweeping new tariffs, including 100% on branded and patented pharmaceutical drugs, to take effect on October 1, with an exemption if the company is actively building a new manufacturing facility in the U.S. 

“The 100% pharmaceutical tariff applies only to branded drugs, and constructing a factory may lead to an exemption. Many pharmaceutical companies have facilities in the U.S., so it may be relatively easy to superficially expand those facilities to avoid tariffs being applied,” UBS' Paul Donovan said.

data.title: Lockheed Martin eyes expansion in Europe, touting Thaad missile defense - Handelsblatt
data.description:
Lockheed Martin (NYSE:LMT) is seeking to market its Thaad missile defense system to European buyers, a top executive told German newspaper Handelsblatt on Friday, as European governments boost military spending in response to Russian threats, including recent incidents involving Russian drones entering NATO airspace.

Lockheed Martin International (NYSE:LMT) head Michael Williamson said in an interview that he sees the greatest growth prospects in markets outside the U.S., such as Europe, over the next five years, and that the company is in negotiations with potential customers for the Thaad system, which costs more than €1 billion.

Thaad could be introduced to Europe as part of the European Skyshield Initiative, a project to build a ground-based integrated European air defense system which includes anti-ballistic missile capability, as an alternative to Israel's Arrow 3 system, according to the report.

Williamson said Lockheed's (LMT) partnership with Rheinmetall (OTCPK:RNMBF) (OTCPK:RNMBY) is a key to securing future contracts in Germany, with Handelsblatt saying the cooperation creates jobs and opens doors to political decision-makers.

Northrop Grumman (NOC), which currently generates 85% of its revenues in the U.S., also sees opportunities in Germany and Europe, with Joseph Guastella, the company's VP leading European operations, telling Handelsblatt that its partnerships in the region "bring benefits for both sides - for Germany and the United States, as well as for NATO as a a whole."

data.title: European indexes rebound as investors shrug off fresh tariff fears
data.description:
Update: The post reflects European markets closing indices.

London (UKX) +0.77% to 9,285. 

Germany (DAX:IND) +0.87% to 23,739. 

France (CAC:IND) +0.97% to 7,871.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) turned 0.78% higher to 554.52, snapping two sessions of losses. Investors largely shrugged off new U.S. tariff announcements from Trump, including a 100% levy on drugs, 25% on heavy trucks and more. Sector-wise, financials led gains while healthcare lagged. 

The European Commission is reportedly planning to impose steep tariffs of 25%–50% on Chinese steel imports and related products in the coming weeks.

In the bond market, the yield on the US 10-year Treasury up 2 basis points at 4.19%.

Germany's 10-year yield was unchanged at 2.75%.

UK's 10-year yield was down 2 basis points to 4.75%. 

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK)

Seeking Alpha Editor Khushi Singh contributed to this article.

data.title: Trump goes after Tylenol again in Truth Social post
data.description:
• President Trump on Friday warned pregnant women not to take Tylenol (acetaminophen) "unless absolutely necessary," repeating comments he made on Sept. 22 at the White House. 
  • In a post on his Truth Social account, Trump also also said Tylenol, which is manufactured by Kenvue (NYSE:KVUE), should not be given to young children "for virtually any reason."
  • The president also repeated his Sept. 22 suggestion that the measles, mumps, and rubella vaccine (MMR) be given as three separate shots, the chicken pox shot should be given separately, and a hepatitis B shot be given only when an individual is 12 years or older. 
  • Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
